name: Negotiation copilot suite
description: Six agents that coach a BUYER through a high-stakes deal negotiation. Demonstrates an anchoring alert that emits an event, a Phase-2 concession tracker that writes facts + a queue, a BATNA reminder on an INTERVAL trigger, a deadline-pressure warning that gets cooldown-gated, and a PRIORITY collision where two agents fight over the same `phase` variable.

agents.name: Phase Tracker
agents.text: You silently track which phase of the negotiation we are in.
agents._doc: Silent low-priority state agent (priority 1). Writes the `phase` variable. It COLLIDES with Deal Strategist (priority 9) on the `phase` variable: on every turn where both write, the engine merges in ASCENDING priority so Deal Strategist writes LAST and wins. Watch the Blackboard `phase` -> it shows the strategist's value ('opening'/'bargaining'/'closing'), NEVER this agent's 'phase_tracker_says_*' value. That is last-write-wins-by-priority. (Both agents read the same rendered transcript, so they always agree on WHICH bucket -- only the written value differs, which is what makes the collision visible.)

agents.name: Deal Strategist
agents.text: You set the authoritative negotiation phase, overriding noisier trackers.
agents._doc: High-priority (9) silent strategist. Also writes the `phase` variable, deliberately colliding with Phase Tracker (priority 1). Because the engine applies updates in ascending priority order, this agent writes LAST and its value ('opening'/'bargaining'/'closing') is the one that lands on the Blackboard. If you lowered this priority below 1, Phase Tracker would win instead -- that is the lesson. Its rules MIRROR Phase Tracker's `when` clauses exactly so the two always select the same bucket and only the written value differs.

agents.name: Anchoring Alert
agents.text: Warn the buyer the moment the seller drops the first number, so they don't accept the anchor.
agents._doc: The anchoring coach. Fires when the SELLER drops the first concrete number. It does THREE things: (1) WARNS the buyer not to negotiate up from the seller's anchor, (2) records an `anchor` fact, and (3) emits the `number_anchored` EVENT. That event wakes the Concession Tracker in PHASE 2 of the SAME turn -- this is the Phase-1 -> Phase-2 one-hop coordination. Cooldown is 8s so a rapid second number is gated (see Deadline lesson).

speak.content: That's their anchor -- the FIRST number always frames the rest. Do not counter just below it. Re-anchor low with your own justified number.

facts.value: seller dropped the first price anchor

agents.name: Concession Tracker
agents.text: Whenever a number is anchored, log it and remind the buyer to trade, never give, concessions.
agents._doc: EVENT-triggered. Runs ONLY in Phase 2, and ONLY on turns where Anchoring Alert emitted `number_anchored`. On every other turn it appears under 'skipped (trigger_type_mismatch)'. When it does run it (1) writes a `concession` fact, (2) pushes a reminder onto the `give_gets` queue, and (3) whispers a suggestion. Watch the Facts + Queues sections of the Blackboard grow only on anchored turns.

speak.content: A number just hit the table. Never concede for free -- every give must buy a get (term, volume, timeline). Log what you'd trade for a discount.

facts.value: seller offer on the table awaiting a counter-with-condition

give_gets: give: longer term -> get: lower per-unit price

agents.name: BATNA Reminder
agents.text: On a periodic tick, remind the buyer of their walk-away alternative (BATNA).
agents._doc: INTERVAL-triggered. It has an EMPTY `when`, so it speaks on every INTERVAL tick the host sends -- but it NEVER fires on a normal turn_based turn, because the engine only routes INTERVAL-subscribed agents on INTERVAL triggers. Find the scenario step with "trigger": "interval": that is the only turn this agent runs. The lesson: trigger TYPE is routing, independent of keywords.

speak.content: BATNA check: your walk-away is the competing supplier at $42/unit. Any deal worse than that is a NO. Negotiate from strength, not fear.

agents.name: Deadline Pressure Detector
agents.text: Catch artificial-urgency tactics and stop the buyer from caving to a fake deadline.
agents._doc: KEYWORD-only trigger. The host (the simulator) detects the keywords and only then routes this agent -- so it is eligible ONLY on scenario steps marked "trigger": "keyword". It WARNS that urgency is usually manufactured. Cooldown is 9s on purpose: when the seller applies deadline pressure twice within 9s (two keyword steps), the FIRST fires and the SECOND is GATED by cooldown -- it appears in 'skipped: cooldown' even though the keyword clearly matched. Because the mode is keyword-only, quiet turns never reset its cooldown, so the two-hit gating is clean and predictable. This is the clearest demonstration that cooldown is a timing gate that is checked AFTER trigger eligibility.

speak.content: Manufactured urgency. A real deadline has a reason; an arbitrary one is a tactic. Slow down -- name the pressure out loud and ask why Friday specifically.
